header: Gerente de People & Culture — Maple Bear
hub_subtitle: Business English para People & Culture, comunicação executiva e a liderança da divisão do Canadá

lesson.menu_title: Diagnosing Culture -- Running a Cultural Assessment with the Canadian Team
lesson.menu_desc: Causative passive e a linguagem de um diagnóstico que a sala aceita: baseline, response rate, root cause, findings -- 36 slides
lesson.subtitle: Aula 6 -- Diagnosing Culture
lesson.title_tag: Professor View -- Danielle Moreira | Aula 6 -- Diagnosing Culture

listenings.text: Danielle, it is Claire. I have your assessment plan in front of me, and before you send it to the faculty I want to change two things in it. The first one is the response rate. You have written that you expect seventy percent. In this division, an anonymous survey has never gone above forty, and the last one came back at thirty-one. If you promise seventy and you deliver thirty, the number becomes the story and your findings never get read. So promise nothing, and tell them instead what you will do if the rate is low. The second thing is the baseline. You have the focus groups in September and the survey in November, and nothing before either of them. That means that in March, when somebody asks you whether the culture has improved, you will have no number to point at. Have the baseline taken first, even a small one, even an imperfect one, and have it taken before you touch anything. And Danielle, one more thing. Whatever the trust index comes back as, it will be lower than the leadership team believes. Do not soften it in the deck. Bring me the root cause underneath it, and bring me two things we can actually do about it.

Danielle, this is Marc. A word before Thursday, because I would rather you hear this from me than discover it in the room. You are about to run a survey in a faculty that has been surveyed before. Two years ago somebody had a very similar assessment carried out here, we all answered it honestly, and then nothing happened. Nobody was ever told what the findings were. So when your questionnaire arrives, the question in everybody's head will not be about the questions. It will be: who reads this, and what happens to it? Answer that first, in writing, or the response rate will tell you a story about us that is not true. And understand what you are doing when you open this. You will surface things that have been quiet for a decade, and some of them are not culture problems at all. They are two people who have not spoken since two thousand and nineteen. That is the can of worms, and it is yours the moment you open it. I am not telling you to close it. I am telling you to know that it is there, and to have a plan for the second week, not only the first.

reading.rtitle: Field note: what a cultural assessment actually costs you
paras: Every People and Culture leader who arrives in a new division wants to run a diagnostic. Very few of them stop to ask what a diagnostic does to the people being diagnosed. The instrument is never neutral. The moment a questionnaire lands in an inbox, the organization starts answering a question you did not write: is it safe to be honest here?

This is why the response rate is the first finding, not a footnote. A team that has had its opinion collected before and never heard the outcome will answer at thirty percent, and the thirty percent who do answer will not be a random sample. They will be the angriest and the most loyal, and the middle of the organization will be missing from your data. A low response rate is not a technical failure. It is a trust index with no survey attached to it.

The second cost is the one nobody plans for. A good diagnostic tool does not collect opinions; it surfaces issues. A focus group that goes well will bring a decade of quiet resentment into a room in ninety minutes, and it will not confine itself to culture. You will open a can of worms, and some of the worms are two colleagues who stopped speaking in 2019. The gap analysis you present to the board will be clean. The room you leave behind will not.

None of this is an argument for not running the assessment. It is an argument for sequence. Have the baseline taken before you change anything, so that in March you can prove what moved. Have the expectations set out in writing before the first question is asked: who will read the findings, when, and what happens to them. And when you present the results, resist the symptom. Attrition is not a root cause. It is what a root cause looks like nine months later.

gist.prompt: Read once, for the main idea. Which sentence says what this text is actually arguing?
choices: Cultural assessments are unreliable, and leaders should trust their own judgment instead of surveys.
A diagnostic changes the organization while it measures it, so the sequence and the promises around it matter as much as the instrument.
Response rates below fifty percent mean the survey should be repeated with a different questionnaire.
Focus groups are more accurate than surveys because they gather insights rather than opinions.

items: The writer says a low response rate is mainly a technical problem with the survey.
It is called a finding in itself: a trust index with no survey attached to it.
According to the text, the people who answer a distrusted survey are not a representative sample.
They are the angriest and the most loyal; the middle of the organization is missing.
The writer argues that the assessment should not be carried out at all.
It is an argument for sequence, not for cancelling the diagnostic.
The text says the baseline should be taken before anything is changed.
So that in March you can prove what actually moved.
The writer treats attrition as the root cause of a culture problem.
Attrition is the symptom: what a root cause looks like nine months later.

items.situation: "Why are we being surveyed again? The last one went nowhere."
tips: That is exactly why I am setting out, in writing, who reads the findings and what happens to them, before a single question goes out.
answer the real question: not the survey, the silence after the last one

items.situation: "Who is actually running this? An outside firm? Us?"
tips: We are having the questionnaire designed by an external specialist, and I am having the focus groups facilitated in-house, because the sensitive conversations should stay in this building.
causative passive: name what YOU arranged, not just what happened

items.situation: "Thirty-one percent answered. Does that not invalidate the whole thing?"
tips: The data would suggest that the response rate is itself the first finding. A team that has been asked before and never told the outcome answers at thirty.
turn the attack into the evidence

items.situation: "So what is the headline? Give me one number."
tips: To some extent, the findings indicate a trust index below the benchmark. But the number is the symptom. What I want to bring you is the root cause underneath it.
hedge the number, then refuse to stop at it

items.situation: "And when do we get all this? Board meeting is in eight weeks."
tips: Our goal is to have the baseline completed by the end of September and the gap analysis presented before the board sits.
the pattern: to have + X + completed by + date

items.situation: "Honestly, are you not opening a can of worms here?"
tips: Almost certainly. Some of what we surface will not be culture at all, and I would rather have it surfaced by us, on a Thursday, than by an exit interview in March.
never deny the risk; own the timing of it
